data.title: China lets fly world’s largest kite in Inner Mongolia for electric power generation
data.description:
China has successfully deployed the world’s largest power-generating kite, capable of harnessing the potential of energy-dense, high-altitude winds to provide cheaper power than traditional surface turbines.
The 5,000 square-metre (53,820 square-foot) high-altitude wind power capture kite was unfurled at a test site at Alxa Left Banner in the Inner Mongolia autonomous region on Wednesday, according to state broadcaster CCTV.
Resembling a parachute, the power-generating kite was developed by the China Energy Engineering Corporation as part of the country’s first national research and development project for high-altitude wind power.
Tethered to a generator on the ground, the kite can fly at an altitude exceeding 300 metres (984 feet).
During the tests, the 5,000 square-metre kite – about the size of 11 standard NBA basketball courts – as well as two connected 1,200 square-metre kites were lifted into the air by helium balloons and successfully deployed and retracted.

Cao Lun, chief commander of the national high-altitude wind project, told state news agency Xinhua on Wednesday that the experiment measured the tension of the kite “in natural wind conditions when it is open and then calculated its opening characteristics”.
Cao said the tests of both types of kites would provide important data for the design and optimisation of a ladder-like wind energy system.
The wind power kites could be deployed at heights exceeding 5,000 metres and cut land use by 95 per cent as well as reduce steel consumption by 90 per cent compared with traditional onshore wind projects, according to CCTV.
They could also lower the cost of electricity per kilowatt-hour by 30 per cent, with one 10-megawatt high-altitude wind power system enough to power more than 10,000 households per year, the state broadcaster said.
High-altitude wind has emerged as a promising renewable energy source, offering enormous generation potential because of the much higher wind speeds and energy density compared with surface wind resources.
The wind energy contained within high altitudes alone could theoretically meet existing global energy demand by a factor of more than 100, according to studies from 2009 and 2012 led by researchers with the Washington-based Carnegie Institution for Science.
Airborne wind energy systems rely on aircraft to harvest wind at high altitudes hundreds or thousands of metres above the Earth’s surface.
These systems can be split into two categories: one in which the electricity generator itself is airborne and another in which the flying parts of the system are used to drive an electricity-generating system on the ground, according to the United Nations.
The cost of the airborne energy harvesting units could be kept low because they are often made of lightweight materials and require minimal use of steel and concrete.
Similar kite systems are being developed elsewhere in the world, including by the Dutch start-up Kitepower, whose kites are flown in continuous cycles of figure-eight patterns to produce energy at the ground station to which they are tethered.
China is also working on developing airborne turbines, with the world’s largest airborne wind turbine, about the size of a basketball court and the height of a 13-storey building, lifting off in September.
The airship-like S1500 became the first turbine of its kind to generate one megawatt of power during a test flight in China’s western Xinjiang Uygur autonomous region.
The turbine floats on helium-filled shells and delivers electricity back down to the ground using heavy-duty cables.

data.title: Meet the Malaysian Muslim trans woman whose 50-year-old secret reshapes history
data.description:
For 50 years, Sari Kartina Abdul Karim kept a secret that would reshape Malaysian history. In 1974, at the age of 26, she became one of the region’s earliest Muslim trans women to undergo gender-affirming surgery and, in a rare moment of religious recognition, married with the approval of a Johor mufti.
For five decades, she kept her story to herself. But now she says that silence has finally outlived its purpose.
“It feels like someone who was blind suddenly being told they have their eyesight again,” she told This Week in Asia in an exclusive interview.
“For 50 years I carried everything inside me. I stayed quiet for my mother and father because I felt responsible for protecting them. Now that generation has passed, I finally feel free to speak.”
Kartina, now 76, lives in the Netherlands, surrounded by the routines of retirement and a small Malaysian community she volunteers with. She spends her mornings with coffee and her two cats, and her afternoons working on a memoir she calls My Fifty Years Journey to Womanhood.

She left Malaysia in the early 1980s, but the defining chapters of her life remain in the southern city of Johor Bahru and Singapore, where she grew up and began the transition that would shape her future.
Her childhood, she recalled, was warm and protected, shaped by a large English-speaking household with colonial-era influences and a constant flow of relatives visiting the family home.
“People imagine someone like me must have had a difficult childhood, but it was fantastic. My mother understood everything about me. I never blamed my parents for anything,” she said.
“The only thing that changed me was when I was 12 and a family member abused me. After that I became introverted. I felt ashamed. But my family still gave me love.”
Whispers are far more painful than the loud insults you hear today
Sari Kartina, who transitioned in 1974
Those early years unfolded at a time when Malaysia barely discussed gender identity. She remembers the 1960s and 1970s as what she calls “an age of whispers”, a period when anyone considered different was talked about softly and often cruelly.
“Whispers are far more painful than the loud insults you hear today,” she said. “When people whisper, you cannot defend yourself. You just pretend nothing is happening and you protect the family’s dignity. That was how we lived. You hide everything under the rug and you behave as if everything is normal.”
In her twenties, she worked in public relations and lived fully as a woman. Around then she met the man she would later describe as “the one who gave me the path to womanhood”.
He accompanied her through six months of psychiatric assessments in Singapore, followed by physical evaluations conducted by Professor S. Shan Ratnam, a pioneer in gender-affirming surgery in Southeast Asia.

“It was very thorough,” she said. “They asked every question they could think of. How do you see yourself? Where do you feel comfortable? What do you do when you need a toilet? All of it was to understand who I really am. I was prepared and inside I was already a woman.”
Because she was Muslim, Ratnam would not operate without religious approval. So Kartina went to the Johor mufti’s office, where she had to explain her identity in detail.
“I had to tell him exactly who I was,” she said. “He looked at me not only physically but mentally. He said he saw a woman. Without that, nothing else would have been possible.”
‘Congratulations, Miss’
Ratnam performed the surgery in 1974. She remembers drifting in and out of consciousness in hospital until one moment came into focus.
“When I woke up, [Ratnam] was standing there,” she said. “He said, ‘Congratulations, Miss.’ I carry that memory with me, always.”
About eight months later, she and her fiancé went to the Johor kadi’s office to formalise their marriage. In Malaysia, Muslim marriages are conducted before a kadi, a state-appointed Islamic registrar who has the authority to decide whether a couple is religiously eligible to wed.
She arrived with her father, brothers and other male relatives as her witnesses. The ceremony was moving ahead without difficulty until her father decided to disclose her past to the kadi.
“Everything stopped,” she said. “We waited while he made calls to the senior clerics. It took hours. My fiancé and I did not speak. We waited for him to decide our future.”

The kadi eventually ruled that she was to be treated as a woman and could be married. Her family held a small reception afterwards.
Reporters were already waiting at the religious office and later turned up at her family home after word spread about what had happened, she said.
She tried to avoid the attention, but her father told her that speaking up might help others like her. She agreed to a single interview with Singapore’s Berita Harian and refused all other requests.
“Malaysia was not ready,” she recalled. “There was no internet, no way to explain myself. I was insulted and people said nasty things about me and my family. People heard the story the way they wanted. It was never my message.”
Her marriage lasted several years before she left for Europe to pursue further studies. The relationship eventually ended. Years later, she remarried in the Netherlands.
“The first gave me the path to womanhood,” she said, her eyes welling. “The second gave me dignity and his name.” Her second husband died three years ago.
Today, Malaysia does not provide a formal mechanism for legal gender change. Muslim trans women can still face prosecution under state Shariah laws for “posing as a woman”, offences that carry fines or jail terms.
Mismatched identity cards continue to cause problems at airports, hospitals and government offices, and the country has no administrative process for changing sex markers.

In Johor, the same state that once approved her marriage, religious authorities have tightened enforcement.
Last year, the Johor Islamic Religious Department received dedicated funding for what it described as counselling and “rehabilitation” programmes for transgender women, alongside expanded outreach and enforcement.
The initiative, which includes a centre that became fully operational in July last year, was framed as a form of help but drew criticism from rights groups who argued it intensified policing of an already marginalised community.
“Malaysia today is still struggling,” Kartina said. “People think being transgender is some kind of trend on television. It is not. It is identity. It is a person claiming back who they really are.
“But I also believe respect goes both ways. The transgender community must respect society and society must respect us. You cannot demand everything if you do not show dignity in how you live … we just want to live as women, as who we are.”
Her faith, she says, has always been her guide. She said: “People say nasty things about me. I always say, Allah knows you. Those people do not. At the end of the day it is between you and Allah. That is how I survived everything.”
When asked whether she saw herself as a pioneer in Malaysia’s transgender history, she paused before answering.
“It is not about pride. It is simply history,” she said. “You cannot erase it. I believe I was the first Muslim woman in Southeast Asia to do this. If someone looks at me and feels courage to continue with their life, then I am honoured.”

data.title: Disney+ reveals what to expect from the year ahead – K-dramas, BTS show, anime and more
data.description:
Hyun Bin, Ji Chang-wook, Park Bo-young and Byeon Woo-seok were among the large contingent of Korean drama heavyweights who lent their star power to this year’s Disney+ Originals Preview event – a showcase of the content planned for the year ahead – at Hong Kong Disneyland on November 13.
Five years after launching its Asia-Pacific content initiative, which has produced more than 150 original projects to date, Disney+ is doubling down on its collaboration with top-tier Asian talent, announcing a star-studded line-up of Korean and Japanese Originals for 2026 and beyond.
“Our role is to give great writers, directors, producers and talent a global platform to tell their stories,” said Luke Kang, president of The Walt Disney Company Asia-Pacific, at the event.
Kang highlighted the growing global demand for Asian content, noting that 60 per cent of its anime titles are watched outside Asia, with Korean storytelling seeing significant growth in new markets such as Brazil and Mexico.

In a recent collaboration with Korean entertainment giant CJ ENM to bring TVing’s Korean content to Disney+ in Japan, as well as the expansion of ESPN on Disney+ in Australia and New Zealand, he said that “things are just getting started”.
“This is a celebration of the creative excellence and the momentum we’re experiencing. It is so exciting for us to be partnering with incredible storytellers who are creating powerful, authentic local stories that can proudly stand alongside global hits.”
The powerhouse Korean slate is headlined by gripping retro crime drama Made in Korea – set in the 1970s and starring A-listers Hyun Bin, Jung Woo-sung and Woo Do-hwan – which will debut on December 24, with a second season already in the pipeline for 2026.
Other crime-action highlights include the now-streaming drama The Manipulated, featuring Ji Chang-wook alongside Doh Kyung-soo; Gold Land with Park Bo-young and Lee Kwang-soo; and the second season of A Shop for Killers, which will see the return of Lee Dong-wook and Kim Hye-jun, with Japan’s Masaki Okada and Korean-Japanese actress Hyunri joining the cast.
Another highly anticipated romantic comedy, Perfect Crown, led by superstar IU as a wealthy heiress and rising heartthrob Byeon Woo-seok as a royal prince.

On the reality television front, the creators of Culinary Class Wars have created Battle of Fates, a competition series pitting fortune-tellers, shamans, psychics and other oracles against each other. Jimin and Jungkook from K-pop super boy band BTS will also be making a return in season two of their lighthearted holiday series, Are You Sure?!, after their military enlistment, which will be released on December 3.
Two major projects turning Korean webtoons into live-action series are also set to debut next year: The Remarried Empress, a messy four-way romance in a fantasy kingdom with Shin Min-a, Ju Ji-hoon, Lee Jong-suk, and Lee Se-young; and Portraits of Delusion, starring Bae Suzy and Kim Seon-ho in a tale about heartbroken vampires.
From Japan, a key bombshell release is a first-of-its-kind collaboration with legendary video game auteur Hideo Kojima. Disney will produce an anime series, Death Stranding Isolations, based on Kojima’s popular post-apocalyptic game. While set in the same universe, the series will tell a completely new story, directed by Takayuki Sano.
Disney Twisted-Wonderland: The Animation and new romantic comedy Merry Berry Love starring widely acclaimed Disney+ regular Ji Chang-wook and Mio Imada are major new releases.
Hit anime continuations like Tokyo Revengers: War of the Three Titans Arc, Cat’s Eye and Medalist will be showing new seasons. There will also be unscripted shows like Travis Japan Summer Vacation!! in the USA and Daigo Project, a new comedy reality show from Daigo of comedy duo Chidori.


Returning international fan favourites include the second seasons of Alien Earth, Percy Jackson and the Olympians, The Artful Dodger, Paradise and the award-winning Shogun. The fifth season of The Bear and the sixth season of Only Murders in the Building are also expected to be released next year. The Descendants franchise will be releasing yet another “happily ever after” twist, titled Wicked Wonderland.
New titles include The Beauty, with Evan Peters and Ashton Kutcher, and The Testaments, an adaptation of Margaret Atwood’s book set after the events in The Handmaid’s Tale.
National Geographic is also featuring some big-name projects from high-profile celebrities, namely Chris Hemsworth’s A Road Trip to Remember, a touching documentary with his father, as well as Will Smith’s Pole to Pole, revealing his travels across the globe.
Eric Schrier, president of Disney Television Studios, said that these collaborations encourage a lot of understanding and growth for different cultures.
“We’re placing bets on people’s dreams, their art and their ability to execute, and then we’re learning from different forms. Each culture has its own distinct sense of storytelling,” he said.
“I think it all needs to be rooted in authentic storytelling. It is so significant for us to continue to make our commitments to the creative community here,” said Carol Choi, executive vice-president of original content strategy for The Walt Disney Company Asia-Pacific. “Everything is grounded in great stories, right?”

data.title: US launches ‘Operation Southern Spear’, deploying robotic fleet to target cartels
data.description:
The United States is launching a major new military and surveillance campaign in the Western Hemisphere, deploying an unprecedented mix of robotic air and sea vessels to counter Latin American drug-trafficking cartels.
Called Operation Southern Spear, the initiative was formally announced on Thursday night by US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth, who said through his X account that the mission followed a direct order from US President Donald Trump.
“President Trump ordered action - and the Department of War is delivering,” Hegseth said. “Operation SOUTHERN SPEAR defends our Homeland, removes narco-terrorists from our Hemisphere, and secures our Homeland from the drugs that are killing our people. The Western Hemisphere is America’s neighborhood - and we will protect it.”
The campaign would be led by Joint Task Force Southern Spear in coordination with US Southern Command and US Naval Forces Southern Command/US 4th Fleet, headquartered at Naval Station Mayport in Florida. Operations were expected to begin later this month.

In a statement, 4th Fleet officials described Southern Spear as a significant step in the US Navy’s evolving Hybrid Fleet Campaign, which integrates robotic and autonomous systems with traditional naval forces.
“Southern Spear will operationalise a heterogeneous mix of Robotic and Autonomous Systems to support the detection and monitoring of illicit trafficking while learning lessons for other theatres,” said Commander Foster Edwards, 4th Fleet’s Hybrid Fleet Director. “This will help develop critical techniques and procedures for integrating RAS into the maritime environment.”
According to the US Navy, the mission will deploy long-dwell robotic surface vessels designed for persistent ocean patrols, small robotic interceptor boats capable of high-speed manoeuvring and vertical take-off, and landing robotic aircraft for aerial surveillance.
The systems will operate alongside US Coast Guard cutters at sea and feed intelligence into operations centres at 4th Fleet and the Joint Interagency Task Force South, which coordinates regional counter-narcotics missions.
Officials say the deployment will help determine the most effective combinations of unmanned vehicles and manned forces for coordinated operations - data that will shape US Navy doctrine under Project 33, the service’s initiative to accelerate robotic integration into fleet missions.
Using robotic assets was expected to increase US presence in maritime chokepoints and high-traffic zones used by drug-smuggling networks. US Navy leaders have said the systems would also strengthen regional security cooperation by giving partner nations access to shared intelligence and detection tools.

“Operation Southern Spear is the next step in our Hybrid Fleet Campaign,” said Rear Admiral Carlos Sardiello, Commander of US Naval Forces Southern Command and US 4th Fleet. “Hybrid Fleet operations increase our collaboration with partners in the region while advancing the Navy’s tactics, techniques, procedures and processes.”
The announcement comes amid a growing military build-up in the Southern Caribbean of a size not seen in the region for decades.
On Tuesday, the world’s largest and most technologically advanced aircraft carrier, the USS Gerald R. Ford, entered the US Southern Command area of responsibility, further expanding a deployment that has alarmed foreign governments and is described by analysts as a potential prelude to strikes on Venezuelan territory, whose leadership is accused by US officials of running the Los Soles drug cartel.
The Centre for Strategic and International Studies has estimated that the US military has more than a dozen vessels in the region, including eight warships, three amphibious ships and a nuclear-powered submarine.
While the operation was officially described as a counter-narcotics mission, the deployment coincided with what officials call “intensifying deliberations” inside the White House over potential direct action against the regime of Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro.
Both The Miami Herald and Wall Street Journal have reported that the administration has identified Venezuelan military installations allegedly linked to drug-trafficking networks as potential bombing targets.

The New York Times has reported that Trump was weighing broader intervention options, including seizing oilfields, targeting Maduro’s elite guard units and even forcibly removing the Venezuelan leader from power.
The administration has not sought a formal declaration of war from Congress. Instead, advisers were exploring alternative legal justifications for regime-change operations under existing anti-drug authorities, arguing that Maduro and his inner circle operate as key nodes in transnational narcotics networks.
Venezuela’s government this week touted a “massive” mobilisation of troops and civilians to defend against possible US attacks. Venezuelan Defence Minister Vladimir Padrino said in a statement that “land, air, naval, riverine and missile assets” would be part of a two-day readiness effort “to confront imperialist threats”.
State television showed members of the military, police and militias standing in formations across the country. Padrino also delivered remarks, broadcast on state television, standing by a surface-to-air missile system in a military base in the capital, Caracas.
Additional reporting by Associated Press

data.title: Malaysia’s Penang orders Chinese tourist’s panda mural removed
data.description:
The Penang Island City Council has ordered the unauthorised mural at a building at Chew Jetty to be removed.
In a notice dated Thursday, issued under Section 82 of the Local Government Act 1976 by the council’s Heritage Conservation Department, the building owner in the Malaysian state was instructed to erase and repaint the wall.
The mural gained fame after a video of a Chinese tourist, known as Shen Shishi, painting a picture of a panda on the mural went viral online.
She was accused of defacing the “Girl on Scooter” mural at Chew Jetty, a historic waterfront settlement built on stilts over the sea.
It was later clarified that the building owner had invited Shen to add the small panda to the existing mural.
Shen was also believed to have painted similar panda figures at several other locations in Penang, including Butterworth.
Chew Jetty, established by Chinese immigrants from Fujian province in the 19th century, is among the last surviving clan jetties in George Town and forms part of Penang’s heritage conservation plan. Its stilt houses and narrow wooden walkways attract thousands of visitors each month.
Under Malaysia’s Local Government Act 1976, anyone who paints, sprays or alters public or commercial property without consent can be fined or prosecuted. In Penang’s state capital, the George Town World Heritage Incorporated oversees such permissions within the Unesco zone.
The murals of George Town became a major attraction after the 2012 George Town Festival, when Lithuanian artist Ernest Zacharevic’s works such as Little Children on a Bicycle turned Penang’s old walls into open-air galleries.
Additional reporting by SCMP’s Asia desk
This story was first published by The Star

data.title: Europe must separate legitimate security concerns from politics
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
As Dutch officials prepare to meet their Chinese counterparts in Beijing to discuss the Nexperia dispute, Europe faces a larger question. How did a routine investment review turn into a diplomatic incident between trading partners who once championed the “rules-based order”?
Western leaders say they are not at war, yet every rule of wartime economics now applies. Company management is seized, trade is blocked and neutral countries are threatened with secondary sanctions if they refuse to take sides. The language remains diplomatic, but the instruments are those of war.
Europe now risks undermining the very legal predictability that once made it attractive to global investors.
Beijing has protested, and it bears restating that every action the Chinese government has taken has been both mild and consistent with Western diplomatic practice. In response to an ongoing series of Dutch export bans and with the addition of this latest takeover, it would not have been unheard of for diplomatic relations to be downgraded.
The Chinese export ban was proportional and moderate, and the Nexperia case shows how easily “de-risking” can become “self-risking” when EU member states act first and coordinate later. Germany’s auto sector and Europe’s semiconductor ambitions both depend on continued market access to China; punitive gestures do nothing to improve security or resilience.
If Europe truly seeks strategic autonomy, it must separate legitimate security concerns from headline-driven politics. A continent built on commercial law cannot preserve its prosperity by rewriting those laws on the fly. Attempts to bully China or other nations because they do not toe the EU line will only result in further incidents. Sovereign nations are not European colonies in this century.
Erik Cota-Robles, Helsinki, Finland
Malaysia is right to challenge court’s damages ruling
I refer to the article, “Malaysia to appeal against damages ruling for families of abducted activists” (November 6).
High Court judge Su Tiang Joo has ordered the Malaysian government and police to pay more than 37 million ringgit (US$9 million) to the family of Pastor Raymond Koh for his enforced disappearance and more than 3 million ringgit to the family of activist Amri Che Mat for negligence in a police investigation.
According to the judge, the compensation to Koh’s family was “for deprivation of personal liberty under the Federal Constitution”, ruling that the federal government was liable for breaching its statutory duty and acting unconstitutionally.
That is constitutional tort. American law professor Marshall Shapo first discussed constitutional tort law – suits brought against government officials for constitutional violations – in a 1965 paper. However, it was not until 1978 that the term was used in the US Supreme Court in Monell v. Department of Social Services, a landmark case that established that local governments can be held liable for constitutional violations that result from their official policies or customs, rather than just the actions of individual employees.
In Malaysia, as noted in the Malaysia report of Civil Liability for Human Rights Violations: A Handbook for Practitioners, while it is arguably open for victims to seek civil remedies for violation of a fundamental right under the Federal Constitution, the application of constitutional tort “has not yet been determined decisively”.
Thus, Malaysian courts have yet to fully endorse infringements of the Federal Constitution as giving rise to a distinct cause of action, with that possibility having only been considered in minority Federal Court decisions in connection with claims for deaths in custody.
The Attorney General’s Chambers said it would challenge the court decisions on Koh and Amri. It should, given that Malaysian courts have yet to fully endorse constitutional tort.
Mohamad Hafiz Hassan, lecturer, Faculty of Law, Multimedia University, Malaysia
Patient records solution close at hand
Your editorial of November 9, “More private-sector buy-in is needed to enhance eHealth”, addresses the sharing of private doctors’ patient records with the Hospital Authority (HA) through the eHealth platform. You noted that some patients were reluctant to give consent to private practitioners for their records to be uploaded.
One way of addressing such issues seems ready to hand. It is common practice for patients to have various tests and scans performed privately in advance of a relatively distant scheduled appointment at an HA hospital.
These patients present themselves at the HA hospital with their test results in hand. Given patient consent during their HA appointment, these results could, on such occasions, be uploaded to the HA system. Perhaps the HA could consider a protocol for implementing such an idea.
Gillian Bickley, Discovery Bay, Lantau

data.title: DNA research suggests Hitler likely had condition limiting sexual development
data.description:
Adolf Hitler most likely suffered from the genetic condition Kallmann Syndrome that can manifest itself in undescended testicles and a micropenis, researchers and documentary makers said on Thursday, following DNA testing of the Nazi dictator’s blood.
The new research also quashes the suggestion that Hitler had Jewish ancestry.
Popular World War II songs often mocked Hitler’s anatomy but lacked any scientific basis.
The findings by an international team of scientists and historians now appear to confirm long-standing suspicions around his sexual development.
“No one has ever really been able to explain why Hitler was so uncomfortable around women throughout his life, or why he probably never entered into intimate relations with women,” said Alex Kay of the University of Potsdam.
“But now we know that he had Kallmann Syndrome, this could be the answer we’ve been looking for,” he said.

The research findings are featured in a new documentary, Hitler’s DNA: Blueprint of a Dictator, due to be broadcast on Saturday.
The testing found a “high likelihood” that Hitler had Kallmann Syndrome and “very high” scores - in the top 1 per cent - for a predisposition to autism, schizophrenia and bipolar disorder, programme makers Blink Films said.
The research team stressed that such conditions, however, could not explain or excuse Hitler’s warmongering or racist policies.
Over 50 million people are estimated to have died in World War II, including 6 million Jews who were systematically murdered.
The testing was made possible after researchers obtained a sample of Hitler’s blood from a piece of material taken from the sofa on which he shot himself.
Kallmann Syndrome often results in “low testosterone levels, undescended testicles and can result in a micropenis”, Blink Films said.
The DNA results additionally rule out the possibility that Hitler had a Jewish grandfather via his grandmother, who was rumoured to have got pregnant by an employer in whose house she worked.
“Analysis of the DNA debunks this myth by showing that the Y chromosome data matches the DNA of Hitler’s male line relative. If he had Jewish ancestry (through an outside relationship), that match wouldn’t be there,” the production company added.
Geneticist Turi King, known for identifying the remains of medieval king Richard III and who also worked on the project, said Hitler’s genes put him in a category of people who were often sent to the gas chambers by the Nazis.
“Hitler’s policies are completely around eugenics,” said the expert in ancient and forensic DNA at the University of Bath in western England.
“If he had been able to look at his own DNA... he almost certainly would have sent himself,” she said.
The two-part documentary is scheduled to begin on the UK’s Channel 4 on Saturday.

data.title: Hong Kong stocks slide most in a week on soft China economic data, fading US rate cut bets
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks fell by the most in a week, triggered by China’s sluggish economic data, uncertainty over an interest-rate cut in the US and lofty valuations of artificial intelligence companies.
The Hang Seng Index slipped 0.9 per cent to 26,833.92 as of 10.21am local time. The Hang Seng Tech Index dropped 1.7 per cent. On the mainland, the CSI 300 Index slid 0.7 per cent and the Shanghai Composite Index retreated 0.2 per cent.
E-commerce giant JD.com slumped 4.1 per cent to HK$119.30 after third-quarter profit fell 55 per cent from a year ago. Peer Alibaba Group Holding dropped 2.8 per cent to HK$157.30. Tencent Holdings shed 2.1 per cent to HK$642.50 even after its quarterly results beat estimates and Meituan lost 1.8 per cent to HK$100.30.
China’s industrial production climbed 4.9 per cent last month from a year earlier, the National Bureau of Statistics said on Friday. The median forecast in a Bloomberg survey was for an increase of 5.5 per cent.
Retail sales gained 2.9 per cent, slowing for the fifth straight month in the longest such decline since 2021. Fixed-asset investment shrank 1.7 per cent in the first 10 months of the year, a record for the period.
Also, the decline in China’s home prices deepened last month.
“Economic activity slowed modestly in October” said Zhang Zhiwei, chief economist at Pinpoint Asset Management in Hong Kong. “The drop of fixed asset investment was quite fast, reflecting the weak property sector investment as well as lacklustre infrastructure investment.”
“Nonetheless, China is on track to achieve the growth target of 5 per cent, so I don’t think we can expect stimulus for the rest of this year. I think fiscal policy support will come in the first quarter.”
US stocks tumbled overnight, with the Nasdaq 100 sliding more than 2 per cent, as a hawkish rhetoric on monetary policy by US Federal Reserve officials pushed the odds of a rate cut next month below 50 per cent. A dimmer outlook for financial easing poses a threat to the crowded trade in technology stocks.
Elsewhere in Asia-Pacific, Japan’s Nikkei 225 slipped 1.5 per cent, South Korea’s Kospi tumbled 2.4 per cent and Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 lost 1.4 per cent.

data.title: Serial intruder grabs Ariana Grande at Wicked premiere in Singapore
data.description:
Cynthia Erivo performed some real magic on Thursday when she saved Wicked co-star Ariana Grande from an overeager “troll” at the premiere of the blockbuster’s second chapter in Singapore.
The Oscar nominees were walking down a yellow brick road-inspired carpet, holding hands with co-star Michelle Yeoh and posing for fans, when a man screamed and hopped the barricade, according to fan footage. The man then wrapped an arm around 32-year-old Grande, only for Erivo, 38, and a guard to cut in.
Erivo then encased the pop star in a protective hug at the Universal Studios Singapore event, as did Academy Award winner Yeoh, 63.
TMZ reported that the man – who was ultimately escorted out by security, encouraged by other fans’ booing – is likely the self-proclaimed “troll” known as Pyjama Man, who posted a 10-second clip of the interaction on TikTok.

Grande is not the first to find herself in the viral clutches of Pyjama Man’s stunts, which tend to involve barging on to a stage, much to the shock of unsuspecting performers, which have included the Weeknd and the Chainsmokers.
Pyjama Man has over 9,300 followers on Instagram, where his profile is verified and his bio proclaims, “Ariana Grande is the Queen”.
Wicked: For Good hits cinemas on November 21, concluding Jon M. Chu’s two-part adaptation of the beloved Broadway musical and Gregory McGuire’s bestselling book from which it takes its name.

data.title: 5 of the best things to do in Hong Kong this weekend, November 14-16
data.description:
The cooler temperatures make for the perfect time to explore Hong Kong’s vibrant outdoor events across the city.
Whether it is a rooftop beer festival, a tournament for a cause, a doggy wonderland or a live street art showcase, there is every way to enjoy the weather this weekend.
Read on to find out more.
1. Tong Chong Street Market Beer Festival












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by mhp by Tong Chong Street Market (@tcsmarkethk)



This Saturday is the last day of the Tong Chong Street Market Beer Festival, taking place on the 37th floor of One Island East in Quarry Bay on Hong Kong Island. The event at the new dining destination, Mhp by Tong Chong Street Market, will offer three hours of bottomless craft beers from eight local breweries, paired with a special festival menu, live DJ sets and beer-themed games.
This year’s festival highlights the vibrancy of the local craft brewing scene with exclusive launches, including the Green Light Pilsner, a honeydew melon-infused brew created for the event and Young Master Brewery’s 12 Shui Wild Sour Ale.
For more information and registration, click here.
Mhp by Tong Chong Street Market, 37/ F, One Island East, Taikoo Place, 18 Westlands Road, Quarry Bay
2. Doggo Fest

Doggo Fest will be taking over the Great Lawn at the West Kowloon Cultural District’s Art Park over the weekend, turning the space into a playground for pups and their owners. The event features a giant ball pit, foam party and photo ops for professional pet portraits. At the same time, human companions can enjoy live music, a dedicated restaurant and nearly 100 vendor booths at the Doggo Expo.
Following an overwhelming early-bird response that saw several sessions sell out, general ticket sales operate on a one-person, one-dog policy, though the expo area remains free. All dogs must present a valid dog license or vaccination record for entry.
For more information and ticketing, click here.
Great Lawn, Art Park, West Kowloon Cultural District
3. Paws United Charity Pickleball Competition












View this post on Instagram

























Paws United Charity (PUC) will host its inaugural PUC Pickleball Charity Competition on November 15 and 16 at the Yama Pickleball Arena. The event aims to rally community support and raise funds for the new PUC Academy, scheduled to open in June 2026. This facility will expand the charity’s capacity for animal rescue, rehabilitation and public education programmes.
The competition will feature performances and participation from a roster of celebrity supporters, including Alfred Hui, Chiu Fung of C AllStar and other top Hong Kong players.
Yama Pickleball Arena, 9/F, Landmark South, 39 Yip Kan Street, Wong Chuk Hang
4. MOS Festival












View this post on Instagram

























The Meeting of Styles (MOS) festival, launched in Germany nearly three decades ago, will be held in Hong Kong for the first time, featuring more than 80 artists from across the globe. Together, they will transform the venue into a dynamic open-air gallery and a landmark moment for the local creative scene.
This weekend will mark the last two days of the five-day programme, where visitors can watch murals created in real time alongside competitive graffiti battles, educational workshops and panel discussions. The event seeks to shift the global dialogue of contemporary urban art, elevating the discourse around street art and offering the public a front-row seat to its creative process.
Rooftop, D2 Place ONE, 9 Cheung Yee Street, Lai Chi Kok
5. Kokuho


Anchored by an astonishing central performance from Ryo Yoshizawa and a fastidious devotion to the traditional art of kabuki theatre, Lee Sang-il’s sumptuous saga Kokuho emerges as the finest Japanese film of the year.
Spanning five decades in the life of a celebrated onnagata – a male performer who specialises in female roles – the film chronicles the endless dedication and heart-wrenching sacrifices required to reach the pinnacle of this bewitching art form, and the toll it takes on the individual.
Earlier this year, Kokuho became the first live-action Japanese film in more than two decades to surpass 10 billion yen (US$65 million) at the box office. Read our full review
Kokuho is in cinemas now.
